question: Which statement best captures why psychology is treated as a science rather than as common sense?

options.A: It depends mainly on intuition developed from everyday life.
options.B: It relies on objective observation, evidence-based testing, and self-correction.
options.C: It accepts explanations that feel personally convincing.
options.D: It focuses only on topics that cannot be measured.

related_info: The lecture defines science as building the best possible models of the world through observation and analysis, and distinguishes it from common sense by emphasizing objectivity, evidence, and self-correction.
why_high_yield: High yield because the introduction lecture uses this distinction to frame the entire course, and Exam 1 explicitly includes research methods.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (research methods explicitly included in coverage)
Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 3-6

question: A researcher proposes that sleep helps memory formation and predicts that sleep-deprived participants will remember less the next day. The prediction is the ______.

options.B: replication

related_info: A theory is a broader explanation, whereas a hypothesis is a specific, testable prediction derived from that theory.
why_high_yield: High yield because theory-versus-hypothesis is a foundational distinction that appears near the start of the research-methods lecture and organizes later examples.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 5
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Why are replications important in psychological science?

options.A: They guarantee that every individual study is correct.
options.B: They help researchers check whether findings hold up across repeated tests.
options.C: They replace the need for theories and hypotheses.

related_info: Replication matters because single studies can be noisy or misleading; repeated successful tests strengthen confidence, which is especially important in the context of the replication crisis.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture highlights the replication crisis as a central reason psychology must be evidence-based and self-correcting.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 6
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: A psychologist measures students' time on social media and their loneliness scores without manipulating anything. This is best described as ______.

options.B: a correlational study
options.D: a case study with experimental control

related_info: Correlational research measures relationships between variables using observational data without manipulating an independent variable.
why_high_yield: High yield because distinguishing correlational from experimental research is one of the main frameworks in the methods lecture and reading.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 8-11
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Why can a correlation between social media use and loneliness not, by itself, prove that social media causes loneliness?

options.B: Because reverse causality or a third variable could explain the relationship.
options.C: Because correlations apply only to laboratory studies.
options.D: Because correlated variables are always unrelated.

related_info: Correlation does not imply causation because the direction may be reversed or both variables may be influenced by another factor.
why_high_yield: High yield because 'correlation is not causation' is one of the most exam-friendly core ideas in introductory psychology.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 10
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: If two variables show a positive correlation, which statement is correct?

options.A: As one variable increases, the other tends to increase as well.
options.B: As one variable increases, the other tends to decrease.

related_info: A positive correlation means the variables move in the same direction, although that still does not establish causality.
why_high_yield: High yield because students are often tested on basic correlation interpretation before being asked to reason about causality.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 9
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which design most directly helps a researcher examine whether changes in X happen before changes in Y?

options.B: Cross-sectional survey

related_info: The lecture notes that longitudinal or prospective studies improve causal inference by checking whether change in one variable precedes change in another.
why_high_yield: High yield because temporal precedence is a classic bridge between correlational evidence and stronger causal inference.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 11
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: A researcher measures parental neglect and statistically removes its influence when examining violent gaming and aggression. This is an example of ______.

options.B: statistical control

related_info: Statistical control strengthens causal inference in correlational work by measuring a possible third variable and adjusting for its influence.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly uses a third-variable example like parental neglect when discussing why correlations are tricky to interpret.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 10-11

question: Which statement best defines a randomized experiment?

options.A: It observes naturally occurring differences without manipulation.
options.B: It manipulates an independent variable and observes its effect on a dependent variable.
options.C: It combines many past studies into one summary statistic.
options.D: It only studies one participant in depth.

related_info: Randomized experiments manipulate the independent variable and compare outcomes on a dependent variable across conditions created by chance assignment.
why_high_yield: High yield because experimental logic is one of the two major designs emphasized in both the lecture and the required reading.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: The purpose of random assignment is to ______.

options.A: make the experimental and control groups initially equivalent on average
options.B: guarantee that no participant drops out
options.C: measure the dependent variable more precisely
options.D: increase the realism of the study setting

related_info: Random assignment places participants into conditions by chance so that participant characteristics can be treated as equivalent across groups on average.
why_high_yield: High yield because the Exam 1 Guidelines use random assignment as a sample multiple-choice question, making this distinction especially likely to be tested.
source_refs: Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (sample question on random assignment)
Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 14
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: In an experiment comparing violent and non-violent games, keeping the room, timing, and food intake the same across groups is an example of ______.

options.B: experimental control

related_info: Experimental control means holding variables irrelevant to the theory constant so that differences in outcomes can be attributed more confidently to the manipulated variable.
why_high_yield: High yield because students are often asked to separate random assignment from experimental control, and the lecture gives a direct gaming example.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 14-15

question: Participants are assigned to either 3 hours of violent gaming or 3 hours of non-violent gaming, and later aggression is measured. In this design, the dependent variable is ______.

options.B: the random assignment procedure

related_info: The dependent variable is the outcome that is measured to see whether it changes as a function of the independent variable.
why_high_yield: High yield because identifying independent and dependent variables is a standard introductory exam skill built directly into the lecture's experiment example.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Why do experiments often have lower external validity than correlational studies?

options.A: Because experiments always use smaller samples than surveys.
options.B: Because experimental settings can be artificial and limited in realism.
options.C: Because experiments cannot include control groups.

related_info: The lecture notes that experiments can be artificial, which may limit how well findings generalize beyond the study setting.
why_high_yield: High yield because introductory exams commonly ask students to trade off stronger causal inference against weaker generalizability.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 16
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which research question is LEAST suitable for a randomized experiment?

options.A: Does a brief mindfulness exercise improve quiz performance?
options.B: Does changing printer defaults reduce paper use?
options.C: Does long-term exposure to neglect predict later aggression?
options.D: Does playing a violent game for one hour affect immediate mood?

related_info: Some variables, such as neglect, lifestyle, or major educational experiences, are impossible or unethical to manipulate and randomly assign.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly says experiments are not always feasible or ethical, which is a classic exam contrast with correlational designs.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 16
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which of the following would count as correlational rather than experimental evidence?

options.A: Randomly assigning people to drink caffeine or water
options.B: Comparing sleep quality and mood using survey responses only
options.C: Manipulating study time in the lab
options.D: Assigning participants to meditation or control groups

related_info: Survey-based measurement of naturally occurring variables is correlational because nothing is manipulated.
why_high_yield: High yield because students need to quickly identify which design is being used from a short scenario description.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 8-12
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: What does a meta-analysis do?

options.A: It repeats a single experiment with the same participants.
options.B: It statistically combines evidence across multiple studies.
options.D: It randomly assigns journals to publish findings.

related_info: Meta-analysis pools results from many studies to provide a broader estimate of an effect, which is especially useful when assessing replicability.
why_high_yield: High yield because meta-analysis is named explicitly in the lecture's discussion of the replication crisis.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 6

question: In a self-correcting science, what should happen when repeated evidence contradicts a theory?

options.A: The theory should be protected from criticism.
options.B: The hypothesis should be deleted but the theory left unchanged forever.
options.C: The theory should be rejected or revised.
options.D: The results should be ignored unless they match common sense.

related_info: The lecture's model of science shows that research with replications should confirm a theory or lead researchers to reject or revise it.
why_high_yield: High yield because it tests whether students understand science as a process rather than a fixed set of answers.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 3-4

question: A psychologist watches children on a playground and records how often they help one another, without manipulating anything. This is best described as ______.

options.B: random assignment

related_info: Naturalistic observation is one form of observational data used in correlational research.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses observational data as a core example of correlational research, and exam items often ask students to identify designs from scenarios.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 8
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which finding would give the strongest support for a causal claim?

options.A: Two variables are correlated in a single survey.
options.B: A manipulated independent variable changes the dependent variable in a randomized experiment.
options.C: An effect seems obvious from personal experience.
options.D: A blog post reports that two events happened around the same time.

related_info: Causal claims are strongest when the researcher manipulates the independent variable, randomly assigns participants, and controls irrelevant influences.
why_high_yield: High yield because many introductory exam questions ask students to choose which evidence best supports causation.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which phrase best describes external validity?

options.A: How well the findings generalize beyond the study setting
options.B: Whether participants were randomly assigned
options.C: Whether the dependent variable was measured accurately
options.D: Whether the study used a control condition

related_info: External validity concerns how well a study's findings extend to other people, contexts, or situations beyond the original design.
why_high_yield: High yield because students are commonly asked to contrast strong internal control with limited generalizability.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 16
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which type of data best fits the lecture's description of machine-learning opportunities in psychology?

options.A: Very small samples with only one variable
options.B: Large-scale behavioral data with many dimensions
options.C: Only participants' introspective diary entries interpreted informally
options.D: Only studies with no numeric data

related_info: The lecture explains that increased computing power and the availability of big, high-dimensional datasets create new opportunities for machine learning in psychology.
why_high_yield: High yield because the course uses computational methods to extend the idea of psychology as a modern empirical science rather than mere intuition.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 17-20
Bonetti et al. (2025). Decoding the elite soccer player's psychological profile.pdf

question: Using text from social media posts to infer political attitudes or emotional tone is an example of ______.

options.B: natural language processing

related_info: The lecture defines NLP as using written language data to analyze human attitudes, emotions, motives, sentiment, and well-being.
why_high_yield: High yield because NLP is one of the clearest examples the introduction lecture gives of new research methods in psychology.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 21-24
Feuerriegel et al. (2025). Using natural language processing to analyse text data in behavioural science.pdf

question: Which use of large language models is most consistent with the introduction lecture?

options.A: Replacing all human theory building with guesswork
options.B: Generating tailored health nudges or simulated practice cases for clinicians
options.C: Making random assignment unnecessary in all experiments
options.D: Eliminating the need for written language data

related_info: The lecture lists applied LLM uses such as generating simulated clinical environments, fine-tuned response examples, and individualized health nudges.
why_high_yield: High yield because LLMs are presented as an especially current extension of psychological measurement, experimentation, and practice.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 25-26
Demszky et al. (2023). Using large language models in psychology.pdf

question: Which change would most improve a correlational study's ability to support a causal interpretation without turning it into an experiment?

options.A: Collecting data at only one time point
options.B: Adding a longitudinal design or statistical control
options.C: Removing measurement of all possible third variables
options.D: Replacing the sample with a more convenient sample

related_info: The lecture specifically lists longitudinal designs and statistical control as ways to improve causal inference in correlational work.
why_high_yield: High yield because it goes beyond memorizing labels and asks students to apply the lecture's causal-inference logic.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 11
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which statement about experimental control is correct?

options.A: It means every participant receives the experimental treatment.
options.B: It means irrelevant variables are kept as constant as possible across conditions.
options.C: It means only control groups are measured.
options.D: It means researchers do not measure outcomes.

related_info: Experimental control reduces competing explanations by holding nonessential factors constant across conditions.
why_high_yield: High yield because students often confuse control with random assignment, and this distinction is directly illustrated in the lecture.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 14-15

question: If a researcher can show that loneliness at Time 1 predicts later increases in social media use, what concern is being addressed most directly?

options.B: external validity

related_info: Longitudinal evidence helps address reverse causality by clarifying which variable changes first.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses social media and loneliness specifically to illustrate how correlation leaves causal direction unclear.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 10-11

question: Which statement about common sense is most consistent with the introduction lecture?

options.A: Common sense is always false, so scientific testing is unnecessary.
options.B: Common sense can feel compelling, but it is not a substitute for objective evidence and self-correction.
options.C: Common sense becomes scientific whenever many people agree with it.
options.D: Common sense and science differ only in writing style.

related_info: The lecture does not claim that common sense is always wrong; rather, it argues that psychology must go beyond intuition by testing claims objectively.
why_high_yield: High yield because this is the central message of the opening lecture and shapes how students should interpret all later topics.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 2-6

question: A researcher wants to study the relationship between years of education and later income, but cannot ethically assign people to vastly different educational histories. Which design is most appropriate?

options.B: A randomized controlled trial assigning education history
options.D: A study with no measured outcome

related_info: When manipulation or random assignment is impossible or unethical, psychologists often rely on correlational designs and then strengthen inference with longitudinal methods or controls.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly uses lifestyle and educational experiences as cases where experiments are not feasible.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 16
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Which scenario best illustrates the lecture's idea that psychology is practically useful once a causal theory is supported?

options.A: A theory shows that changing X can change Y, allowing intervention on Y by manipulating X.
options.B: A survey reports two variables are related, so one automatically becomes a treatment.
options.C: A hypothesis remains untested, but practitioners apply it anyway.
options.D: Researchers avoid measuring outcomes to preserve realism.

related_info: The lecture explains that causal theories matter because once a theory is supported, people can take practical action by changing the cause to influence the outcome.
why_high_yield: High yield because it connects abstract methods to applied psychology, a repeated design emphasis in the course outline.
source_refs: Course Outline SOSC1960-L1 (Spring 2026).pdf (course design emphases)
Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 5

question: Which research feature most directly protects psychology from becoming a collection of memorable but unreliable anecdotes?

options.B: Using only striking examples

related_info: Replication helps separate robust effects from one-off findings or persuasive anecdotes by requiring that results recur under repeated testing.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture links replication directly to the credibility of psychological science.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 6

question: If loneliness actually leads people to spend more time on social media, which problem for causal inference does this illustrate?

options.B: reverse causality

related_info: Reverse causality means the direction of influence may run opposite to the one initially assumed, which is one reason correlation alone cannot prove causation.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture uses social media and loneliness to illustrate exactly why correlational findings are causally ambiguous.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 10-11
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Suppose violent gaming and aggression are correlated only because both are linked to poor parental supervision. Poor parental supervision would be a ______.

options.B: conditioned response

related_info: A third variable is an outside factor that may influence both measured variables and create a misleading correlation.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture explicitly uses parental neglect as a third-variable example when warning that correlation is not causation.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 10
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: A correlation coefficient of -0.85 would be described as ______.

options.B: a weak negative relationship

related_info: The minus sign indicates direction and the large absolute value indicates strength, so -0.85 is a strong negative correlation.
why_high_yield: High yield because correlation questions often test whether students can separate direction from strength.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 9

question: In the violent-game experiment described in the lecture, the independent variable is ______.

options.B: the type of game assigned
options.D: the amount of noise in the room

related_info: The independent variable is the factor deliberately manipulated by the researcher, in this case whether participants play a violent or non-violent game.
why_high_yield: High yield because identifying independent versus dependent variables is a standard exam move in research-design questions.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15

question: What is the main purpose of including a control condition in a randomized experiment?

options.A: To provide a comparison baseline for judging the effect of the manipulation
options.B: To guarantee that the experiment has high external validity
options.C: To replace the need for random assignment
options.D: To make the study correlational rather than experimental

related_info: A control condition gives researchers a comparison point so they can judge whether the manipulated condition changed the outcome.
why_high_yield: High yield because control conditions are central to experimental logic but are often confused with experimental control or random assignment.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: A school delays start time for all students and compares attention scores before and after the change, but students were not randomly assigned. This is best described as ______.

options.B: a quasi-experiment

related_info: A quasi-experiment includes a manipulation or naturally occurring change but lacks random assignment, so causal inference is weaker than in a true experiment.
why_high_yield: High yield because the required reading broadens research-design comparisons beyond the basic correlational-versus-experimental distinction.
source_refs: Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)
Exam 1 Guidelines.pdf (research methods and required readings included)

question: Asking participants to answer questions about their own stress, sleep, or attitudes is an example of using ______ data.

options.B: experimental-control

related_info: The lecture lists surveys as one of the main observational tools used in correlational research.
why_high_yield: High yield because students are often asked to classify everyday study designs from short method descriptions.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 8
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: Using existing hospital records or school files that were originally collected for another purpose is an example of ______.

options.B: random assignment

related_info: Archival data come from records that already exist and can be analyzed as observational evidence without manipulating variables.
why_high_yield: High yield because archival data are one of the three concrete examples the lecture gives under correlational research.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 8

question: Which statement is LEAST likely to qualify as a good scientific hypothesis?

options.A: Students who sleep after learning will remember more than students who stay awake.
options.B: People assigned to a mindfulness exercise will report less stress than controls.
options.C: Violent gaming will increase aggression on a later task compared with non-violent gaming.
options.D: Human behavior improves whenever the universe decides it should.

related_info: A good hypothesis must be specific and testable; vague supernatural claims are not easily testable in scientific research.
why_high_yield: High yield because it reinforces the lecture's insistence that scientific claims must be testable rather than merely appealing.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 5

question: Why are multi-lab studies especially useful when researchers worry about replicability?

options.A: They allow the same effect to be tested across multiple settings and teams.
options.B: They guarantee that every study will support the theory.
options.D: They turn correlational findings into causal ones automatically.

related_info: Multi-lab studies strengthen confidence in findings by checking whether effects appear across different samples, researchers, and contexts.
why_high_yield: High yield because the lecture names multi-lab studies alongside replication and meta-analysis in its discussion of scientific credibility.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 6

question: Training a cross-validated algorithm to classify elite soccer players from psychological scores is an example of ______.

options.B: machine learning applied to behavioral data
options.C: archival data only with no model testing

related_info: The introduction lecture uses the soccer-player example to show that machine learning can classify people from large sets of psychological features.
why_high_yield: High yield because it is the most memorable concrete example in the computational-methods section of the lecture.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 18-20
Bonetti et al. (2025). Decoding the elite soccer player's psychological profile.pdf

question: Using blog posts or other text to identify signs of depression, anxiety, or suicidal thinking is presented in the lecture as an example of ______.

options.A: natural language processing for well-being research
options.B: operant shaping

related_info: The NLP section lists well-being detection from written language as one way text analysis can support psychological research.
why_high_yield: High yield because it shows how modern methods can measure core psychological outcomes from real-world data.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 21-24
Feuerriegel et al. (2025). Using natural language processing to analyse text data in behavioural science.pdf

question: Generating personalized health nudges and then testing whether different people respond differently is described in the lecture as one potential use of ______.

options.B: sensory adaptation

related_info: The lecture explains that LLMs could generate individualized nudges and help researchers test heterogeneous treatment effects in health contexts.
why_high_yield: High yield because it connects a current AI example to the lecture's larger point that psychology keeps expanding its research toolkit.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 25-26
Demszky et al. (2023). Using large language models in psychology.pdf

question: Which label is essentially another name for a randomized experiment in the lecture and required reading?

options.B: naturalistic observation

related_info: The lecture notes that randomized experiment, true experiment, and randomized controlled trial refer to the same basic design logic.
why_high_yield: High yield because exams often test whether students recognize equivalent methodological labels.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, p. 12
Scollon, C. N. (2023). Research Designs (required reading)

question: In research design, a manipulation refers to ______.

options.B: randomly sampling participants from the population

related_info: A manipulation is the deliberate change to the independent variable so the researcher can observe whether the dependent variable changes in response.
why_high_yield: High yield because manipulation is one of the most basic elements separating experiments from correlational studies.
source_refs: Introduction/Lecture Slides - isn't psychology just common sense.pdf, pp. 12-15
